target.name: KoBold Metals
target.founder: Kurt House, Josh Goldman, Jeff Jurinak

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $2.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 8 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $120M - $6.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
KoBold Metals is a Series C climate and energy company currently valued at $2.0B post-money, having raised $1.0B+ to date. The valuation analysis draws from a peer universe of eight same-sector, same/adjacent-stage private companies—including Electric Hydrogen ($1.0B Series C), Amogy ($700M Series C), and Redwood Materials ($6.0B Series D)—plus one public comparable (Linamar, ticker LNZA).

Two triangulation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis applied a 1.7x multiplier to the $2.0B last post-money valuation, implying a forward value of $3.4B. Peer-median analysis across the eight-company cohort yielded a median valuation of $450M, though the range spans $120M to $6.0B, reflecting significant heterogeneity in the peer set by stage and capital efficiency. The peer median sits materially below KoBold's last round, likely reflecting the sample's inclusion of earlier-stage and smaller-scale entrants.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $382M (low) to $3.9B (high), with a median of $1.9B. This range brackets the current $2.0B valuation within the upper-middle band. Key uncertainties include KoBold's progress on near-term commercial validation relative to peers, capital requirements to reach production scale, and commodity price exposure embedded in copper and nickel exploration assets. The wide peer dispersion ($120M–$6.0B) underscores the stage-dependent and risk-contingent nature of valuations in this sector.
